title: The Quantum Hamiltonian Analysis Toolkit: Lowering the Barrier to Quantum Computing with Hamiltonians
authors: Brendan K. Krueger, Stephan Eidenbenz, Shamminuj Aktar, Rishabh Bhardwaj, John K. Golden, George Grattan, Abhijith Jayakumar, Anna Matsekh, Scott Pakin, Nandakishore Santhi, Reuben Tate
abstract: We present the Quantum Hamiltonian Analysis Toolkit (QHAT), a newly developed application that provides a user-friendly interface for studying Hamiltonians and performing Hamiltonian simulation on fault-tolerant quantum computers. QHAT enables the generation and analysis of Hamiltonians through a powerful and feature-rich application, driven by simple inputs designed to reflect user needs rather than algorithmic details, so that productive research on your application of interest can be done without needing a deep understanding of quantum computing algorithms.   QHAT enables a streamlined workflow to analyze Hamiltonians and Hamiltonian simulation, supporting multiple choices of algorithms and analyses. It supports Hamiltonians from multiple sources but can also generate Hamiltonians based on a simple description of the system, saving intermediate data files for re-use when generating related Hamiltonians. Deriving the parameters for quantum computing algorithms can be a challenge, so QHAT is built around user-facing concepts such as maximum allowable error, rather than being built around algorithmic details such as steps counts or order parameters. An emphasis on user-friendly interfaces and efficient analysis means that the barrier to entry is low while rapidly providing results useful for a broad scope of studies.
summary: The paper introduces QHAT, a new software toolkit designed to bridge the gap between physical chemistry/materials science and fault-tolerant quantum computing. It automates the complex process of converting physical system descriptions into optimized quantum algorithms and resource estimates based on user-defined error tolerances. This lowers the barrier for non-experts to explore the practical utility of quantum hardware for Hamiltonian simulation.
main_problem: The high barrier to entry for subject-matter experts to use fault-tolerant quantum computing for Hamiltonian-based applications due to the difficulty of translating physical requirements into low-level algorithmic parameters.
main_result: The development of QHAT, a Python-based toolkit that automates the workflow from Hamiltonian generation to resource estimation, allowing users to define simulations via high-level error tolerances rather than low-level algorithmic details.
method: An end-to-end software framework that integrates existing packages (pySCF, OpenFermion, Qualtran, pyLIQTR) to automate Hamiltonian encoding (Trotterization, block encodings), algorithm construction, and resource estimation.
model_or_system: The toolkit is demonstrated using molecular Hamiltonians (e.g., H2O, Li-Li) and spin models (XXZ Heisenberg), focusing on second-quantized electronic structure and fermion-to-qubit mappings.
key_observables: T-gate counts, Clifford gate counts, qubit requirements, circuit depth, and state fidelity.
important_parameters: Maximum allowable error, energy error tolerance, evolution time, active space size, and Trotter step counts.
main_assumptions: The toolkit currently focuses on molecular Hamiltonians and assumes the use of Hartree-Fock for initial state generation; error bounds for higher-order Trotter expressions are noted as poorly constrained.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate the water molecule geometry, the software workflow, the quartic scaling of Pauli strings with active space size, resource estimation plots (qubits vs. T-gates), and the sensitivity of Trotter error to term ordering.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QHAT toolkit, describes its design principles for subject-matter experts, details the integrated workflow for Hamiltonian generation and encoding, presents scaling analysis and resource estimation results, and concludes with future development directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Geometry of our example molecule, water. Purple data describes the shape of an H2O molecule. Green coordinates are used by QHAT.
Fig. 2: Diagram of Hamiltonian generator. Each step fur- ther specializes the data, which means that intermediate data can be used to generate related Hamiltonians.

Fig. 3: Diagram of composite algorithm model. Each red ˆUA and blue ˆUB is identical so only a single instance of each needs to exist or be analyzed. The same is true of purple ˆU, except that (a) the ˆU operator itself is common and a relatively smaller amount of work can extend the analysis to the various different controlled- ˆU operators, and (b) ˆU p is simply ˆU repeated p times.

Fig. 4: Diagram of QHAT workflow, showing the variety of options available (green) and in progress (yellow). Additional features are in the planning stage, but not shown here. A simple description of the desired algorithm, with the option to override default behavior, enables QHAT to construct a model and perform the requested analyses. These results can then be used for further numerical or theoretical study of the Hamiltonian and/or algorithm in question.

Fig. 5: Hamiltonians generated by QHAT (Section VI-B) showing each configuration as a single line where the size of the active space is varied to produce differ- ent Hamiltonians. The shaded region is bounded by y = 0.01x4 and y = 0.1x4, showing that the asymptotic increase in the number of Pauli strings is quartic in the size of the active space. This behavior is consistent with the rank-four tensor that expresses the two-body interactions in a second-quantization Hamiltonian.
